name: Big Five / NEO-PI-R (Costa & McCrae)
core_claim: The Big Five (Five-Factor Model) claims that adult personality variance can be summarised by five broad, largely orthogonal dimensions: Openness to Experience, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism (OCEAN). The NEO-PI-R is the canonical operationalisation, measuring each of the five domains plus six facets per domain (30 facets) via self- or observer-report. It is the most psychometrically validated and cross-culturally replicated personality taxonomy in psychology, derived from lexical analysis of trait adjectives and confirmed by factor-analytic and behavioural-genetic work. The theoretical link to media preference rests on Uses-and-Gratifications and Mood-Management theory: stable traits shape what gratifications a person seeks from media. Openness is the load-bearing trait for HelpME2C purposes — it indexes intellectual curiosity, tolerance for ambiguity, aesthetic sensitivity and preference for novelty/complexity, which maps directly onto narrative complexity, genre breadth and willingness to cross into unfamiliar (e.g. foreign-language, art-house) content. Extraversion (preference for high-arousal, social, upbeat content), Neuroticism (mood-regulation viewing, sad/comfort content), and to a lesser extent Conscientiousness and Agreeableness contribute smaller, more specific signals. The model's relevance is that personality is observable BEFORE any viewing history exists, making it a candidate cold-start prior.
predictive_validity_for_media: The empirical link is real but the effect sizes are modest and trait-specific. Predicts genre/thematic preference: Openness is the strongest and most consistent predictor — it correlates with preference for complex, unconventional and aesthetic content (documentaries, art-house/independent film, fantasy, philosophy/serious fiction); Rentfrow & Goldberg (2011, Journal of Personality, large US samples) derived five entertainment-preference factors (Communal, Aesthetic, Dark, Thrilling, Cerebral) and found the Aesthetic factor correlated strongest with Openness (with smaller positive Agreeableness and slight negative Conscientiousness loadings). Typical domain-level correlations are small: most |r| in the 0.10-0.30 range, and music-preference meta-analytic coefficients were near zero with only ~6 of 30 exceeding |r|>=0.10. Extraversion predicts preference for upbeat/social/high-arousal and Communal/Thrilling content; Neuroticism predicts mood-management/sad-film and escapist use; sensation-seeking (correlated with low Conscientiousness and aspects of Extraversion/Openness) predicts thrilling/dark/horror content. Predicts narrative-style preference: Openness predicts tolerance for ambiguity, nonlinear and slow-burn forms (moderate, mostly cross-sectional evidence). Predicts completion rate: weak/indirect evidence — Conscientiousness plausibly relates to finishing series but this is largely untested for streaming. [uncertain] Overall: personality explains a small but non-trivial slice of preference variance (single-digit to low-double-digit percent of variance), best treated as a weak prior, not a strong predictor. Designs are overwhelmingly cross-sectional self-report correlational; few longitudinal or behavioural (actual-watch) validations exist.
instrument_design: Full NEO-PI-R: 240 items, 5-point Likert, ~30-45 minutes — far too long for onboarding. Validated short forms exist along a length gradient: NEO-FFI (60 items, ~10-15 min); BFI-44 (44 items, ~5 min); and the minimal forms relevant here — BFI-10 (Rammstedt & John 2007, 10 items, 2 per domain, <1 minute, English and German, retains acceptable reliability/validity) and the Ten-Item Personality Inventory (TIPI, Gosling et al. 2003, 10 items, 7-point, two bipolar adjective pairs per domain). Both 10-item forms have low internal consistency by design (alpha ~0.40-0.73) but strong convergent validity with full measures (r ~0.65-0.87) and adequate test-retest reliability. There is also the BFI-2-XS (15 items) and single-item-per-domain measures (SIMP). Minimum viable version for HelpME2C: a 10-item BFI-10/TIPI-style instrument is the realistic floor at 2 items/domain. If only the media-relevant traits are needed, Openness + Extraversion + Neuroticism could be captured in 6 items (2 each), under ~60-90 seconds, sacrificing Conscientiousness and Agreeableness which carry the least media signal. Response format is Likert/agreement, which is the friction-and-bias weak point (see quiz_ux_implications).
cold_start_applicability: This is the model's main strength: it requires zero viewing history and produces signal at t=0, before the first interaction. A 6-10 item quiz yields a stable trait estimate that can seed a content prior immediately. However the signal quality is LOW-to-MODERATE in absolute terms — because trait-to-genre correlations are small (mostly |r|<0.30), personality alone cannot rank specific titles; it can at best tilt a broad genre/theme prior (e.g. nudge an Openness-high user toward complex/foreign/slow-burn content and away from formulaic content). It works for cross-medium inference in principle (the trait-to-preference link is medium-agnostic and Rentfrow showed shared structure across music, film and books), making it suitable for HelpME2C's TV+anime cross-medium goal — but the effect is a weak, broad-strokes prior, not a substitute for even a handful of explicit title ratings. Best framed as a Bayesian prior that the first few interactions rapidly overwrite, and as a tie-breaker/diversity-shaper rather than a primary ranker. [uncertain]
group_compatibility_use: Directly relevant to HelpME2C's two-person core. Two routes exist. (1) Aggregate predicted preferences: convert each person's traits to a content prior, then aggregate the two priors using standard group-recommender strategies — Average (AVG, maximises mean satisfaction), Least-Misery (LM, minimises the worse-off person's dissatisfaction — well-suited to a two-person 'something we both tolerate' goal), Most-Pleasure, or Average-Without-Misery (AVG with a veto floor). The group-recommender literature (Masthoff; Springer GRS chapters) finds LM and AVG are the two dominant strategies; LM tends to do better for small groups and for avoiding a strongly dissatisfied member, which matches a couple/dyad joint-watch goal. (2) Combine personalities directly: trait-similarity or complementarity between the two profiles. Evidence on which combination predicts JOINT satisfaction is thin and mostly outside media — most personality-pair research is on relationship satisfaction, not joint media enjoyment, so the link is inferential. [uncertain] Recent work (e.g. arXiv 2304.08851 'Personality-Guided Preference Aggregator for Ephemeral Group Recommendation') uses personality to weight members' influence in aggregation, which is a promising pattern: a more-Open or more-assertive member's preference could be up-weighted, or conversely down-weighted to protect the less-flexible member. Recommendation for HelpME2C: aggregate at the preference-prior level (not the trait level) using Least-Misery-with-a-floor as the default joint strategy, since the North Star is a show BOTH want to watch; treat trait-similarity as a secondary explanatory signal, not the aggregator.
quiz_ux_implications: Likert self-report is the default elicitation but carries the classic biases. Acquiescence bias (yea-saying) is material for short scales — BFI-10/TIPI partly mitigate it by using reverse-keyed items, which is essential to include. Social-desirability and faking bias are LOW in this context because there is no incentive to fake for a recommendation quiz (unlike hiring), so honest answering is plausible — this is a meaningful advantage over employment use. The bigger UX risk is boredom/abandonment and the 'this feels like a personality test, not a fun onboarding' framing. Mitigations: gamify/disguise as preference-and-scenario questions ('Would you rather...' forced-choice, image/poster A-vs-B, or short situational vignettes) rather than naked 'I see myself as someone who...' agreement statements; forced-choice/ipsative formats also reduce acquiescence and scale-use response styles, at the cost of producing relative not absolute scores (a trade-off worth it here since we only need a tilt, not a clinical score). Keep to 6-10 items, one screen, progress indicator, ~60-90 seconds. Frame as 'help us get your first picks right' not 'discover your personality'. Avoid Neuroticism items that read as clinically probing (could feel intrusive and trip the privacy concern below).
cross_cultural_validity: The strongest available model cross-culturally, but not free of breakdowns relevant to HelpME2C's multilingual catalog. The five-factor structure replicates broadly across major world regions including East Asia, Europe and the Americas (McCrae & Terracciano; Schmitt et al. 2007, 56 nations) — so Korean, Japanese, French, German, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese and Chinese contexts are reasonably well covered with validated translations of NEO/BFI. Known weak spots: Openness is the least stable factor cross-culturally and the hardest to translate (its lexical markers vary most by language); congruence drops in some African and South/Southeast Asian samples (Schmitt reported lowest Openness congruence ~0.53-0.56 in Botswana and Nigeria; India, Morocco also <0.90), and the model fails to replicate cleanly in some small-scale/non-literate societies (e.g. Tsimane). For HelpME2C this matters because (a) Openness is precisely the trait we most want, and it is the most translation-fragile; (b) Hindi and Arabic content audiences fall partly in the lower-congruence zone, so a single-language English quiz auto-translated could degrade signal; (c) response-style differences across cultures (extreme vs midpoint responding) bias Likert scores independent of true traits. Implication: use professionally validated localisations of any items rather than machine translation, and treat the personality prior as weaker/optional for lower-congruence locales. [uncertain]
privacy_consent_implications: Personality data is privacy-sensitive and edges toward GDPR Article 9 special-category territory. Personality scores are not themselves an enumerated special category, BUT under ICO/EDPB guidance inferred data becomes special-category if you intentionally draw or act on inferences about health (e.g. Neuroticism read as mental-health status), beliefs, or political opinions. A Neuroticism/emotional-stability score in particular is plausibly inferable to mental health, raising Article 9 risk. Requirements: a lawful basis under Article 6 (consent is the cleanest for a voluntary onboarding quiz, and unlike employment it can be freely given here) AND, if any item touches special-category inference, a separate Article 9 condition (explicit consent). Best-practice technical architecture (per the search guidance) is to design so sensitive inferences are not generated at all — i.e. avoid Neuroticism items, or store only a derived content-prior vector rather than raw trait scores, minimising re-identification and sensitive-inference risk. For HelpME2C specifically this is a Stop-and-Ask / ADR-0012 zone: collecting personality data is processing for profiling, needs explicit opt-in consent, a clear purpose-limited statement, and should be hard-deletable on account deletion. Prefer transient use (compute prior, discard raw answers) or store the minimal derived vector with explicit consent. Coordinate with the privacy-control flow before implementing.
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Mapping is strongest for Openness and clearest as a tilt across the texture and structure categories. High Openness -> nonlinear-storytelling, slow-burn, earned-ambiguity (note: not an existing slug; closest existing are nonlinear-storytelling / meditation-on-time / character-study), magical-realism, meditation-on-time, meditation-on-death, character-study, satire-of-power, absurdist-comedy, cosmic-horror, alternate-history, first-contact, dystopian-society; and openness to foreign-language/art-house content generally. Low Openness -> preference for conventional, resolved, formulaic structure: whodunit, workplace-procedural, heist-and-caper, underdog-rise, clear redemption-arc. High Extraversion -> communal/upbeat/high-energy: ensemble-mosaic, found-family, friendship-tested, heist-and-caper, chase-and-escape, celebrity-and-fame. High Neuroticism -> mood-regulation and emotionally intense/cathartic: grief-and-loss, loneliness-and-isolation, trauma-survival, mental-illness-portrayal, unrequited-longing, existential-horror (but avoid pushing distressing content to high-Neuroticism users without care). High sensation-seeking (low Conscientiousness + Openness facets) -> chase-and-escape, survival-and-endurance, existential-horror, cosmic-horror, post-apocalyptic-survival, vigilante-justice, rise-and-fall. High Agreeableness -> relationship/prosocial warmth: found-family, platonic-soulmates, redemption-arc, mentor-and-pupil, coming-of-age; lower appetite for toxic-relationship, complicity-with-evil, power-corrupts. High Conscientiousness -> structured, moral-order content: workplace-procedural, mystery-investigation, institutional themes, and plausibly higher completion of long-form serialised narratives. These are directional priors with small effect sizes — useful for shaping diversity and tie-breaking, not for confident per-title ranking. [uncertain]
implementation_recommendation: PARTIAL include. Recommendation: include a 6-item subset measuring Openness, Extraversion, and Neuroticism only (2 items each, reverse-keyed pair within each trait), drawn from validated BFI-10/TIPI item sets, presented as a gamified forced-choice or 'would you rather'/A-vs-B poster format rather than naked Likert agreement. Place it as an OPTIONAL, skippable step in onboarding AFTER (or alongside) a few explicit title/genre seed picks, never as the sole cold-start mechanism — because effect sizes are small, personality is a weak prior that explicit seeds outperform. Use it as: (1) a fallback prior when the user gives almost no explicit seeds, (2) a diversity/tie-breaking shaper, and (3) the basis for the two-person joint aggregation (Least-Misery-with-floor over the two derived priors). Drop Conscientiousness and Agreeableness from the quiz (lowest media signal, more items = more friction). Drop or carefully reframe Neuroticism if the Article 9 / sensitive-inference review (ADR-0012) does not clear it — Openness + Extraversion (4 items) is a safe minimal core. Dependencies: most valuable when combined with explicit genre/theme seed picks; on its own it is a weak signal. Cross-medium and group use are the strongest justifications for including it at all. Requires: explicit opt-in consent, professionally localised item translations for the supported languages, and storing only a derived content-prior vector (not raw trait scores) where possible. North-Star trace: a personality-seeded joint prior lets us recommend a show two people with no shared history both want to watch on day one — directly serving the metric.
key_sources: Rentfrow, P.J., Goldberg, L.R., & Zilca, R. (2011), 'Listening, Watching, and Reading: The Structure and Correlates of Entertainment Preferences', Journal of Personality 79(2) — free full text: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2964424/ and https://projects.ori.org/lrg/PDFs_papers/RentfrowEtal2011EntertainmentPreferencesJP.pdf (FREE). Costa, P.T. & McCrae, R.R. (1992), NEO-PI-R Professional Manual (BOOK, paywalled/proprietary instrument). Gosling, S.D., Rentfrow, P.J., & Swann, W.B. (2003), 'A very brief measure of the Big-Five personality domains' (TIPI), Journal of Research in Personality 37 (widely available FREE via gosling.psy.utexas.edu). Rammstedt, B. & John, O.P. (2007), 'Measuring personality in one minute or less: A 10-item short version of the BFI in English and German', JRP 41 — https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0092656606000195 (PAYWALLED; preprints on academia.edu FREE); GESIS BFI-10 https://www.gesis.org/en/services/planning-studies-and-collecting-data/items-scales/bfi-10 (FREE). Schmitt, D.P. et al. (2007), 'The Geographic Distribution of Big Five Personality Traits', Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology — http://toddkshackelford.com/downloads/Schmitt-JCCP.pdf (FREE). McCrae & Terracciano (2005), 'Personality Profiles of Cultures' / Five-Factor Model Across Cultures (FREE summaries). Group recommender aggregation: Masthoff, 'Group Recommender Systems: Aggregation, Satisfaction and Group Attributes', Springer https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4899-7637-6_22 (PAYWALLED); 'A Personality-Guided Preference Aggregator for Ephemeral Group Recommendation' arXiv:2304.08851 https://arxiv.org/pdf/2304.08851 (FREE). GDPR: ICO special category data guidance https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/lawful-basis/a-guide-to-lawful-basis/special-category-data/ (FREE).
